items.q: When addressing a stranger or someone older formally, which 'you' do you use?
items.correct: Sie (always capitalised, formal singular and plural).
items.why: Students default to du. German has THREE 'you' forms: du (informal singular), ihr (informal plural), Sie (formal singular OR plural - capitalised). Using du with a stranger or older person is rude. Sie takes third-person plural verb forms.

items.q: How do you say 'I watch TV every evening' in German?
items.why: Students keep separable verbs together. FERNSEHEN splits in main clauses: sehe...fern. The prefix 'fern' goes to the END of the clause. Same with anrufen (rufe...an), aufstehen (stehe...auf), einkaufen (kaufe...ein).
Students assume gender follows meaning. German gender is grammatical, not biological. The diminutive suffix -CHEN makes any noun neuter, regardless of natural gender. So das Mädchen, das Mädel.

items.q: What is the correct reflexive pronoun: 'ich verstehe ___ gut mit meinen Eltern'?
items.why: It's mich. “mir” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: When was Ehe für alle introduced?
items.why: It's 2017. “2001” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Up to how many months of Elterngeld can a couple receive (basic Elterngeld)?
items.why: Exact answer: 14 months. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
